left.title: House Democrat calls for congressional hearings on Trump administration’s nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia – as it happened

left-center.title: With Saudi Deal, Trump Loosens Nuclear Standards for One Close Ally

center.title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia

farLeftText: From 10h ago 19.34 EDT Hearing echoes of US military support for Shah of Iran, Democratic congressman calls for hearings on Saudi nuclear deal At House foreign affairs committee hearing on Wednesday, congressman Brad Sherman, a Los Angeles Democrat, called for an oversight hearing of the Trump administration’s nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, suggesting that exporting that technology could be as big a mistake as having armed the Shah of Iran in the 1970s. “There are those who think that the Saudi possession of nuclear technology is totally benign, but I’ve been involved in this for a long time, and I remember when the most powerful monarch in the Middle East was a good friend of America. And the Shah got his hands on important US military technology. Thank God, nothing nuclear,” Sherman said while questioning Mike Waltz, the US ambassador to the UN, and former national security adviser to Donald Trump. “Everything the Shah had in 1978 was in the mullahs’ hands in 1979,” Sherman said, referring to the Islamic revolution that toppled the Iranian monarch and brought the theocratic regime to power. “We have no idea who is going to be running Saudi Arabia 10 years from now.” Video of congressman Brad Sherman questioning UN ambassador Mike Waltz on Wednesday at a House foreign affairs committee hearing. Sherman went onto point out that, in 2018, he and then-senator Marco Rubio had introduced the “No Nuclear Weapons for Saudi Arabia Act” which would have required approval from Congress before the US could enter into a civilian nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, and a Saudi investigation into the death of journalist Jamal Khashoggi - whose murder the same year in Turkey US intelligence later determined had been approved by the Saudi crown prince, Mohammed bin Salman. Sherman argued that Congress should approve the deal only if it has what he called later “the same gold-standard safeguards as our deal with the UAE,” referring to a similar agreement with the United Arab Emirates struck in 2009, in which the UAE voluntarily committed to nonproliferation and agreed to not pursue domestic uranium enrichment and reprocessing. “Is there any reason we should be bombing Iran because they won’t agree to limit their nuclear program while telling Saudi Arabia we give them the green light and they don’t even have to agree to these additional inspections?” Sherman asked Waltz. After Waltz argued that US resolutions calling on Iran to not develop nuclear weapons provided a pretext for the current US-Israeli war on Iran, Sherman suggested there was “great hypocrisy here.” “Saudi Arabia is not agreeing to the additional inspections that could make sure that their program is not used … to develop a nuclear weapon,” the congressman said, based on press reports of the terms of the agreement that has not yet been shared with Congress. “I predict someone will introduce a resolution of disapproval and I invite members of this committee who have worked with us to prevent nuclear weapons in the Middle East, from non-democratic countries, to join in that effort,” Sherman said, in an oblique reference to the open secret that one nation in the region, Israel, does have nuclear weapons. Share Updated at 19.50 EDT 7h ago 22.00 EDT Closing summary This concludes our live coverage of the second Trump administration for the day. Here are the latest developments: The Trump administration signed a nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia on Wednesday that could enable the kingdom to enrich its own uranium for nuclear reactors. At a House foreign affairs committee hearing, congressman Brad Sherman , a Los Angeles Democrat, called for an oversight hearing of the Trump administration’s nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, suggesting that exporting that technology could be as big a mistake as having armed the Shah of Iran in the 1970s. The US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-party-line vote, after Democrats refused to back the measure amid the war with Iran and Donald Trump’s push to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide. During his 75-minute address to supporters in Georgia, Trump took some time out from boasting about what he calls his many accomplishments to attack Jon Ossoff , the incumbent Democratic senator he referred to, confusingly, as “Pinky Herman”. As the US strikes again targets in Iran in retaliation for that nation’s blockade of the strait of Hormuz, Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen claim to have attacked two Saudi ⁠oil tankers for violating a blockade near the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, a chokepoint for oil exports transiting the Red Sea. Share Updated at 22.42 EDT 7h ago 21.54 EDT Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed does have Black support, AOC says, after Clyburn suggests he does not Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, the progressive New York congresswoman, denied on Wednesday that the Michigan Senate candidate she supports, Abdul El-Sayed, lacks support from Black voters. The congresswoman was asked to respond to a comment her Democratic colleague James Clyburn made to the outlet Punchbowl about images of an El-Sayed rally in Detroit on Saturday that she and senator Bernie Sanders spoke at. Local news photographs, social-media images and video of the rally at the Detroit Opera House shared online showed that the crowd, in a city that is 75% Black, was largely white. “I saw a picture that concerned me coming out of Michigan. I saw the rally,” Clyburn told Punchbowl. “I didn’t see a single person that looks like me. That tells me all I need to know.” Some of the Black voters who did attend the rally told Sam Robinson, a Black independent journalist, that they were not troubled by online criticism of a majority white crowd in a majority Black city supporting El-Sayed. Denzel McCampbell, a Black member of Detroit’s city council who is campaigning for El-Sayed, dismissed what he called “a narrative about this campaign, about Abdul” created by “folks on Twitter”, and insisted that he has seen support for the candidate while knocking on doors in the city. “His message is something that resonates with folks across a broad spectrum,” McCampbell said. At least one recent poll suggests that El-Sayed’s rival in the Democratic primary for Senate, congresswoman Haley Stevens, leads with Black voters. She is also supported by at least a dozen members of the Congressional Black Caucus, including Clyburn. “I think when you look on the ground, Abdul El-Sayed enjoys the support locally of many Black elected leaders and Black community leaders throughout the state of Michigan,” Ocasio-Cortez told reporters on Wednesday. “I also think the community is nuanced. I don’t believe that it’s a monolith. I think, just like many other communities, there are generational dynamics that are to be considered,” she added. “So we’ll see as the race plays out.” One of the rally speakers before El-Sayed was a Black elected official: Donavan McKinney, a Democratic Socialist state lawmaker who is running for Congress. View image in fullscreen Donavan McKinney, Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, Abdul El-Sayed, Bernie Sanders, Shawn Fain and Rashida Tlaib on stage at a campaign rally for El-Sayed in the Detroit Opera House on 18 July. Photograph: Jose Juarez/AP Clyburn’s comments come amid a struggle inside the Democratic party to decide which states should go first in the 2028 presidential primary campaign, with his home state of South Carolina vying with states including New Hampshire, Nevada and Michigan to hold the first primary. Share Updated at 22.51 EDT 9h ago 20.41 EDT UAE agreed not to enrich uranium in nuclear deal with Obama; will Trump's deal with Saudis be different? On its face, the nuclear cooperation agreement, commonly known as a 123 agreement (after the relevant section of the US Atomic Energy Act) signed on Wednesday by the Trump administration and Saudi Arabia looks similar to an agreement signed in 2009 by the Obama administration and the United Arab Emirates. However, until we see the text, it is not clear if the deals are alike in one key way: whether or not the Saudis have agreed, as the UAE did, to safeguards that would block the kingdom from developing nuclear weapons. In the UAE deal, first negotiated during the Bush administration, and then reworked and signed by the Obama administration on 21 May, the Emirates agreed to accept the International Atomic Energy Agency’s so-called “additional protocol”, which allows the agency to verify the peaceful use of all nuclear material, and affirmed its commitment “to pursue a renunciation of the development of domestic enrichment and reprocessing capabilities in favor of long-term commitments for the secure external supply of nuclear fuel”. “In signing this Agreement, the UAE has demonstrated its commitment to develop civil nuclear energy responsibly, in full conformity with its nonproliferation commitments and obligations,” the Obama state department wrote in a 2009 fact sheet. “Among those is the UAE’s commitment not to pursue enrichment and reprocessing (ENR) within its territory, representing a marked contrast to Iran which has failed to comply with its international obligations.” Initial reports on the terms of the Trump administration’s deal with Saudi Arabia suggest that it could allow for uranium enrichment in Saudi Arabia, opening a potential pathway to the production of nuclear weapons in the kingdom. “The deal breaks from the ‘gold standard’ of nonproliferation, which would prohibit domestic uranium enrichment and plutonium reprocessing,” the Wall Street Journal editorial board argued on Wednesday, under the headline: “Saudi Nuclear Enrichment Is a Mistake”. The Journal’s editors went on to suggest that Trump’s Saudi nuclear deal will have weaker safeguards than the Bush-Obama UAE nuclear deal: double quotation mark The next best way to prevent nuclear proliferation is with inspections and monitoring, but the Trump deal weakens those, too. The tried-and-true method is the International Atomic Energy Agency’s Additional Protocol. The United Arab Emirates signed up for it, along with the gold standard of no domestic enrichment, as a condition of its 2009 nuclear deal negotiated largely with George W. Bush. But now the Saudis have been allowed to reject the Additional Protocol and receive U.S. support all the same. The Trump Administration says it has devised a bilateral safeguards agreement of its own. But we doubt the Saudis rejected the standard protocol in favor of even stricter inspections. They are bound to be weaker, establishing the precedent that each U.S. ally can push for its own preferential monitoring. Share Updated at 20.46 EDT 9h ago 19.49 EDT George Chidi Donald Trump scoffed at the word “affordability” as a political message, describing it at a campaign rally on Wednesday as a term concocted by Democratic political consultants to criticize his presidency. Yet even as the president dismissed the notion of affordability, he devoted much of his speech at Wheeler high school to touting the bread-and-butter benefits of his economic policies. “So I just want you to remember that if you’re paying a little bit more – a lot of it’s come down now, we got it coming down very rapidly actually,” Trump said in Marietta, an Atlanta suburb, casting higher prices today as a lingering effect of the Biden administration. Americans are broadly disappointed with Trump’s handling of the economy, with just 33% of voters approving in a recent Washington Post-Ipsos poll, an assessment that could hurt his party’s hopes of bucking historical precedent and retaining control of Congress in November’s midterm elections. Trump scoffs at ‘affordability’ as he touts economic policies at Georgia rally Read more Share 10h ago 19.39 EDT Bernie Sanders calls deal to provide nuclear technology to Saudi Arabia 'absurd' Senator Bernie Sanders, a Vermont independent, expressed strong opposition to the Trump administration’s deal to help Saudi Arabia enrich uranium for nuclear energy, a capability that could open the door to the repressive kingdom developing nuclear weapons. “Trump claims his war on Iran is about stopping an authoritarian country from enriching nuclear fuel,” Sanders wrote on social media. “Now he’s letting his best friends in Saudi Arabia — one of the most brutal dictatorships in the world — do exactly that. This is absurd. Share Updated at 19.50 EDT 11h ago 18.41 EDT George Chidi At a Donald Trump event in Marietta, Georgia, the state’s Republican candidate for governor Rick Jackson pledged to ask the Georgia legislature to match the $1,000 federal contribution to childhood Trump investment accounts, and if they failed to do so, would match the figure himself. “Here in Georgia, we should not stope with $1000,” Jackson said. “I believe our legislature will approve that. If they don’t I will personally donate it myself.” Jackson is the founder of Jackson Healthcare, a medical staffing company. His personal net worth has been estimated to be more than $3 billion. In 2024, the state of Georgia recorded a bit more than 126,000 live births, which equates to $126 million in annual contributions for Trump accounts. Georgia began the 2026 fiscal year with an unprecedented budget cushion of about $15 billion in the bank, with $9 billion in unrestricted funds. This surplus resulted from conservative budgeting that began in 2021 as a reaction to pandemic pressures. That money has become an intense political question, with Republicans looking for ways to provide property tax relief while Democrats arguing for a broader investment in health care and child care support. Share 11h ago 18.31 EDT As the US strikes again targets in Iran in retaliation for that nation’s blockade of the strait of Hormuz, Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen claim to have attacked two Saudi ⁠oil tankers for violating a blockade near the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, a chokepoint for oil exports transiting the Red Sea. Saudi Arabia, which borders Yemen, led a military intervention in 2015, triggering a year-long civil war to prop up Yemen’s Saudi-backed government. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations Centre said in an alert issued on Wednesday night it “has received a report of an incident 70NM southwest of AL SHUQAIQ, Saudi Arabia. The Master of a tanker reports being struck by an unknown projectile which has caused a fire onboard that the crew are currently fighting.” Senator Chris Murphy, a Connecticut Democrat, responded to news of the Houthi attacks by writing on social media: “Everyone saw all of this coming. This chaos was totally foreseeable and Trump chose to start this war anyway.” Share Updated at 20.54 EDT 11h ago 18.16 EDT Trump boasts about helping defeat Jon Ossoff in 2017 special election, fails to mention his role in Ossoff's Senate win in 2021 During his 75-minute address to supporters in Georgia on Wednesday, Donald Trump took some time out from boasting about what he calls his many accomplishments to attack Jon Ossoff, the incumbent Democratic senator whose withering criticism of the president has thrilled Democrats and led to calls for Ossoff to mount a 2028 presidential campaign, should he first win his reelection bid in November. “I fought Ossoff before, as you know, and we won. We had a great success against this guy,” Trump said, fondly recalling his role in Ossoff’s narrow loss in a 2017 special election in Georgia’s sixth congressional district, in what was then a Republican-dominated Atlanta suburb. “He reminds me of Pinky Herman,” the president said. “Do you ever see Pinky Herman?” Trump asked, a comment that prompted some laughter from his supporters in the hall, and a scramble, among journalists, to figure out what, exactly, he was talking about. It was not until fully thirteen minutes later, when Trump predicted that Ossoff’s Republican challenger, Mike Collins, is “going to beat Pee-wee Herman”, that the 80-year-old president’s reference to the character played by the late comic actor Paul Reubens four decades ago became clear. For a president who rose to power largely through his insult-comic hits on his political opponents, it was a peculiarly low-energy jibe, given that Ossoff, who was once as skinny as the Reubens character, has now bulked up, and speaks about the president in a tone of righteous indignation that bears no resemblance to Pee-wee’s squeaky voice. Trump made no mention at all of the prominent role he played in trying, and failing, to stop Ossoff from winning his Senate seat in a run-off election on 5 January, 2021. The night before that election, in which Democratic senator Raphael Warnock also won his seat, Trump had traveled to Georgia to speak at a rally for the two Republican candidates, senators Kelly Loeffler and David Perdue. Rather than urging voters to support the Republican candidates, Trump instead undermined confidence in the electoral system in remarks focused mainly on his claim that the presidential election just two months earlier had been stolen from him. “Hello, Georgia! By the way, there is no way we lost Georgia. There’s no way, … That was a rigged election” Trump began his speech that night. “When you win in a landslide, and they steal it, and it’s rigged, it’s not acceptable,” he told the crowd, hours before Republican turnout slumped and Ossoff and Warnock were elected, swinging control of the Senate to the Democrats. View image in fullscreen Donald Trump with then-senator Kelly Loeffler at a rally in Dalton, Georgia on 4 January 2021, the night before Republicans lost a pair of Senate runoffs as Trump claimed that elections in the state were “rigged”, despite being administered by Republican officials. Photograph: Sandy Huffaker/AFP/Getty Images Share 12h ago 17.21 EDT US announces nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could enable kingdom to enrich uranium The Trump administration signed a nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia on Wednesday that could enable the kingdom to enrich its own uranium for nuclear reactors, US Energy secretary Chris Wright said in a press release. According to a statement from the Department of Energy, Wright and his Saudi counterpart, Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, signed “a peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement” and “an accompanying bilateral safeguards agreement.” The question of what those safeguards look like, given the potential for enriched uranium to be used to make nuclear weapons, will be closely scrutinized when the agreement is sent to Congress for review. The two agreements, the press release said, “lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation.” “These agreements reflect our two nations’ shared commitment to strengthening U.S.-Saudi commercial relations, delivering prosperity at home and security to our allies abroad,” Wright said in a statement. “Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world’s best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States.” As our colleague Patrick Wintour reported earlier: double quotation mark The timing of the announcement is controversial as the US’s war with Iran is being fought in part to prevent Tehran enriching uranium domestically and make it near-impossible for Iran to build a nuclear weapon. The US-Saudi deal is expected to last 30 years and involve US firms such as Westinghouse in developing the programme. It could allow for the building of a uranium enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia after a joint US-Saudi study. The proposal has been in discussion for years. At one point the Biden administration wanted to condition Saudi Arabia’s access to the technology on normalising relations with Israel, something Saudi Arabia is still refusing to do. The plan is expected to be submitted to Congress for review, where it could face opposition among lawmakers who fear a lack of monitoring safeguards to restrict Saudi Arabia’s uranium enrichment. Share Updated at 17.28 EDT 12h ago 16.46 EDT Chris Stein The US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-party-line vote on Wednesday, after Democrats refused to back the measure amid the war with Iran and Donald Trump’s push to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide. A key plank of the president’s plan to spend $1.5tn on the military in the 2027 fiscal year, the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA), passed the Republican-controlled chamber by a 216-212 vote, with nearly all Democrats opposing its passage. The measure now goes to the Senate. Congress passes the NDAA each year with bipartisan margins, with the measure’s high cost seen as necessary to make generational investments in the military. “For the first time in 40 years, we’ve been presented a budget that accounts for the true cost of American deterrence. This topline begins to reverse the damage caused by decades of underinvestment in the US military,” Mike Rogers, the House armed services committee chair, said before the vote. But the path to enactment for this year’s NDAA has been complicated by the president’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without first seeking authorization from Congress. Last week, Senate Democrats blocked the advancement of that chamber’s version of the NDAA in protest of the war, which has flared anew after a memorandum of understanding meant to spur peace talks collapsed. US House passes $1.15tn defense policy bill in near party-line vote Read more Share 13h ago 16.36 EDT Trump rails against specter of 'communism' to supporters in Georgia Donald Trump is currently delivering what is supposedly a presidential address to promote a new domestic policy, namely the investment accounts for children he calls Trump accounts, but, surrounded by supporters in Marietta, Georgia, his remarks quickly became deeply partisan, repeatedly attacking Democrats and falsely claiming the party is now filled with “communists”. Trump, whose political mentor Roy Cohn led senator Joseph McCarthy’s 1950s witch hunts for imaginary communist infiltrators in the US government, made the wild claim that Democrats he called “communists by another name” want to blow up Mount Rushmore. He then proclaimed: “we declare again today that the United States of America will never be a communist country!” The crowd, led by a group of die-hard Trump supporters who call themselves Front Row Joes, for their repeated presence at his rallies, broke into a chant of “USA! USA!” View image in fullscreen Donald Trump dances after speaking at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Georgia, on 22 July. Photograph: Saul Loeb/AFP/Getty Images Share Updated at 17.05 EDT

centerText:
The US and Saudi Arabia have signed a landmark deal that will allow the Gulf kingdom to develop a civilian nuclear programme.

The deal is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy program" by the US energy department.

Text from the deal has not been made public, but US media report that it could allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium in the future - which can be used as fuel for power plants, but also to make nuclear bombs.

Experts say that would mark a first for the US and could run the risk of future nuclear proliferation in an already unstable region.

The US Department of Energy said a "bilateral safeguards agreement" had been signed along with the co-operation deal.

"Together, these two agreements lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation," the statement said.

The Saudi government also released a statement saying the deal "represents an extension of existing co-operation between the two countries in the energy sector", noting that it comes after the Saudi leader visited Washington last November.

topic: Mamdani Arrest Netanyahu Enforce Warrant Mayor

left.title: Mamdani says he can’t arrest Netanyahu as mayor and urges US to enforce ICC warrant

left-center.title: Mamdani: NYC Doesn’t Have ‘Legal Authority’ To Enforce ICC Arrest Warrant Against Netanyahu

farLeftText: Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, doubled down on his support for the international criminal court (ICC) on Wednesday and called on federal authorities to execute the court’s warrant for Benjamin Netanyahu’s arrest should he visit the city in the fall. “I call on the federal government to join the international criminal court and to execute both this warrant as well as any warrant the international criminal court holds,” Mamdani told reporters at a press conference on Wednesday. Those remarks came less than 24 hours after Mamdani called the Israeli prime minister a “war criminal” who is “not welcome in New York City” in a video statement posted on social media. In the statement, the mayor acknowledged he had no legal authority to direct New York police to arrest Netanyahu should he attend the United Nations general assembly in September. Mamdani had previously said he was “in conversation” with city lawyers to determine whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu, as he had pledged to do during his mayoral campaign last year. On Wednesday, Mamdani rejected arguments from critics that Israel’s war crimes in Gaza have nothing to do with New York City. Israel’s actions in Gaza, he said, had “offended the conscience, the morals, the mere humanity of so many New Yorkers”. Mamdani’s statements about Netanyahu were remarkable for a US municipal leader and a signal of how support for Palestinian rights remains a signature policy for him. His insistence that the US government arrest Netanyahu has been met with condemnation and ridicule from pro-Israel officials. Pro-Palestinian advocates’ reactions were more mixed – some praised the mayor’s Tuesday statement for its “moral clarity” while others expressed disappointment that he walked back on his promise to seek the Israeli leader’s arrest. “I think the mayor should stay in his lane,” Jonathan Harounoff, Israel’s former international spokesperson at the United Nations, told NY1, accusing him of “political theater”. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania, one of the most staunchly pro-Israel Democrats in the Senate, called Mamdani a “clown” who should “sit down”. “America’s not even part of that corrupt court,” Fetterman said in an interview with Fox News over the weekend. “That’s really not your purview.” Simcha Eichenstein, a pro-Israel New York state assemblymember, had previously called on the state attorney general to review whether Mamdani’s comments about Netanyahu violated state law. The ICC issued arrest warrants against Netanyahu and the former Israeli defense minister Yoav Gallant in 2024, accusing them of war crimes and crimes against humanity, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”. “I believe Prime Minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani told the New York Times last week, referring to the city in the Netherlands that is home to the court. Neither the US nor Israel are members of the ICC, which the US government has long opposed and which Marco Rubio, the secretary of state, this month threatened to “dismantle” by pressuring other countries to abandon it. Mamdani said he concluded his administration had no authority to execute the ICC arrest warrant, but shifted the burden on the Trump administration to do so. skip past newsletter promotion Free newsletter | Around 2-3 times a day Sign up to Breaking News US Get the most important news as it breaks Preview latest Enter your email Sign up after newsletter promotion “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant,” he said in Tuesday’s statement. “The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.” Donald Trump has already said he will do no such thing. Netanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil, the president said in a social media post earlier this week, adding that the Israeli prime minister was “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”. Netanyahu had said in the past he was “not concerned” with Mamdani’s threat, and his office said in a statement last week that the mayor “appears interested in diverting public attention from his follies and attacking the leader of the Jewish state and the only democracy in the Middle East”. It called the ICC a “kangaroo court”. Supporters of the court have also praised Mamdani’s efforts to stand up for the tribunal’s mission. “My mayor is doing what any NYC mayor would do if Vladimir Putin or any other indicted war criminal planned to visit NYC,” said Adil Haque, a legal scholar. “He is exploring every legal avenue and exhausting all legal options. If his hands are tied, he will use his voice.”
centerLeftText: LOADING ERROR LOADING Mayor Zohran Mamdani on Tuesday acknowledged the limitations of New York City’s legal authority when it comes to enforcing an arrest warrant against Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, but noted that the federal government doesn’t face the same constraints. “My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City can execute the International Criminal Court’s arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here. It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant,” Mamdani said in a video posted to X. “The federal government, however, does, and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.” Advertisement During his mayoral campaign, Mamdani pledged to order Netanyahu’s arrest, and he recently told The New York Times that he was in active discussions with his office’s legal department about what the law would allow him to do. In the wake of that interview, President Donald Trump vowed that the Israeli leader, who could visit New York City this fall for the U.N. General Assembly, would not be arrested in the U.S. in “any way, shape, or form.” Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal. pic.twitter.com/YRezmW6YVx — Mayor Zohran Kwame Mamdani (@NYCMayor) July 22, 2026 Advertisement In 2024, the ICC issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu and accused him of crimes against humanity, which he denied. That warrant stated that Netanyahu was “allegedly responsible for the war crimes of starvation as a method of warfare and of intentionally directing an attack against the civilian population” as part of Israel’s military campaign against Gaza. The U.S. is not a party to the Rome Statute, which established the ICC, and does not observe its authority. The Trump administration has also taken a more hostile posture toward the ICC, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio recently launching a campaign aimed at dismantling the body. Mamdani, who described Netanyahu as “the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” also detailed why the prime minister should be arrested. Advertisement “He is responsible for the killing of more than 73,000 people, for the maiming of tens of thousands of children, as those who survive undergo amputation without anesthesia. For the targeting of neonatal hospitals and maternity care centers, denying newborns even the chance to live. For the countless people he starved as he blocked food and humanitarian aid from reaching them. For the gunning down of hundreds of aid workers and journalists,” Mamdani alleged in his video on Tuesday. “And just last month, the U.N. confirmed that Palestinian children continue to be deliberately targeted and killed by Israeli armed forces more than eight months after the so-called ceasefire.” “I agree with the ICC that Benjamin Netanyahu should be arrested and tried for his crimes, as I do for anyone else charged by the ICC,” Mamdani added. In a statement on Sunday, Netanyahu’s office attacked the ICC, calling it a “kangaroo court,” and criticized Mamdani in response. Advertisement “Mr. Mamdani should focus on fixing the damage his policies have caused New York,” the statement said. It also claimed that Israel had “taken unprecedented wartime measures to minimize harm to civilians while confronting Hamas.”

topic: Criminal Referral Jack Smith Laughably Weak

left.title: 'Laughably weak': GOP's criminal referral against Jack Smith dismissed as 'meaningless'

left-center.title: House Judiciary issues criminal referral against Jack Smith over alleged perjury

right-center.title: Jack Smith hit with criminal referral as GOP moves testimony fight toward prosecution

farLeftText: The House Judiciary Committee is referring former Special Counsel Jack Smith for criminal prosecution, but analysts are dismissing it. On Wednesday, Rep. Jim Jordan (R-OH) alleged that Smith "knowingly made false statements" and referred him to the Department of Justice for prosecution. Jordan, the chairman of the House Judiciary Committee, alleged in a letter to Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche that "strong evidence" appears to show Smith lied during a December 17 testimony. Smith is the prosecutor who brought two now-dismissed criminal cases against Trump relating to 2020 election interference and the retention of classified documents at Mar-a-Lago. The December 17 testimony mentioned in Jordan's letter refers to a closed-door House Judiciary deposition where Smith defended prosecuting Trump. Despite Jordan's strongly worded criminal referral against Smith, legal analysts, journalists, and Democratic lawmakers are dismissing it. Rep. Jamie Raskin (D-MD), the ranking member of the House Judiciary Committee, slammed Jordan's criminal referral in a public statement. Raskin was particularly upset with Jordan for faulting Smith for not volunteering National Archives and Records Administration presidential information that Republicans never asked about. CNN political analyst Xochitl Hinojosa noted, "Trump called for Jack Smith's prosecution, so DOJ spent 20 months trying to find ANYTHING on Jack and failed," she wrote in a post on X. Around the time news of the referral came out, Politico legal reporter Kyle Cheney simply reposted what he wrote in 2025: "Periodic reminder that criminal referrals from Congress, other than for contempt, are substantively meaningless."

centerLeftText: Washington — President Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth attended a dignified transfer at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware on Wednesday honoring the four U.S. Army soldiers killed in the Middle East in recent days during the ongoing war with Iran. The Trump administration is investigating attacks at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan that led to the deaths of two of the soldiers, and the presumed death of one other who was initially described as missing. The dignified transfer also included an Army sergeant killed at a base in northern Iraq during a controlled detonation of an Iranian drone. The service members who were killed were: Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Hawaii Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Texas Sgt. Angel Rampersad, 28, of New York Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, of North Carolina The White House said the president met with each of the soldiers' families. President Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth salute during a dignified transfer for Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad at Dover Air Force Base in Dover, Delaware, on July 22, 2026. SAUL LOEB /AFP via Getty Images "We're going to Dover," the president told reporters before attending the transfer. "Some of you are going with us, to honor our heroes, and they are indeed great heroes, actually. They said, and they — all of them said very strongly — we can't let, we cannot let Iran have a nuclear weapon. They won't have a nuclear weapon. So we're going to honor them. And it's one of the hardest things to do. For me, it's one of the hardest things to do as a president. But, has to be done." Feehan and Gonzales were both killed in action during the strikes on the base in Jordan, according to the Pentagon. U.S. Central Command had announced Rampersad as missing in action, but updated her status Tuesday to whereabouts unknown, and she is believed to have been killed in the same attack. Swinton, a father of two, was killed at Erbil Air Base in northern Iraq during the detonation of an Iranian drone. From left: Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas; Sgt. Angel Rampersad, 28, of Ozone Park, New York; 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii; and Sgt. Michael Swinton, 30, of Fayetteville, North Carolina. Department of Defense The president told reporters Wednesday that the administration would share the results of the probe into the attacks in Jordan. A dignified transfer is a nonceremonial movement of a fallen U.S. service member killed in a theater abroad back to the United States. The fallen are moved in flag-draped cases, not caskets, and carried by members of the military. It is customary for the president or other high-ranking administration officials to attend.

US SOLDIER KILLED IN JORDAN ATTACK REMEMBERED AS LIVING ‘LIFE OF COURAGE AND SELFLESSNESS’

RUBIO ACCUSES IRAN OF FLYING IRGC PERSONNEL INTO YEMEN AS HOUTHI THREAT ESCALATES

U.S. Central Command said over the weekend that the service members were killed at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan as CENTCOM and partner forces "defended against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks."

Swinton was killed while troops attempted to destroy a downed Iranian attack drone Sunday at an air base in Iraq.

President Donald Trump attended on Wednesday the dignified transfer of the four servicemembers killed in the Middle East from Friday into the weekend.

The president and other distinguished visitors observed the dignified transfer of 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25; Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad, 28; Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30; and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware.

Feehan and Gonzales were killed in action following Iran’s ballistic missile and drone attack on Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan on Friday. Rampersad, who was announced as missing after the attack, is believed to have been killed during the assault, according to the Department of War. Swinton was killed on Sunday during the controlled detonation of a downed one-way attack drone at Erbil Air Base in Iraq.

All incidents are under investigation.

Feehan hails from Ewa Beach, Hawaii; Gonzales from Carrollton, Texas; Swinton from Fayetteville, North Carolina; and Rampersad from Ozone Park, New York.

On his way to the Air Base, Trump said receiving the remains of America’s fallen is “one of the hardest things to do as a president.”

Upon arriving at Dover Air Base, Trump met with the families of the fallen before the dignified transfer, according to the White House press pool.

The president, standing next to Secretary of War Pete Hegseth, saluted each transfer case as it was transported from the C-17 to a vehicle on the Air Base.

Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Dan Caine and, according to the White House press pool, a dignitary who appeared to be acting Chief of Staff of the U.S. Army Gen. Christopher LaNeve, Chief of Naval Operations Adm. Daryl Caudle, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt, and executive assistant to the president Natalie Harp were on hand for the transfer.

The president departed for Georgia after the event, and Feehan’s family traveled aboard Air Force One with him.

“The family of LT Tyler Feehan will be flying to Georgia with us. They are from Georgia, and the president offered to give them a ride home,” a White House official said.

On Monday, Trump pledged that Iran will pay “many times over” for each American service member it kills.

topic: Police Madison Wisconsin Sparking Protests Officer

left.title: Police officer fatally shoots man in Madison, Wisconsin, sparking protests

left-center.title: Man shot and killed by police in Madison, Wisconsin, sparking protests

farLeftText: A police officer in Madison, Wisconsin, shot and killed a man Wednesday in an incident that was captured on cellphone footage, sparking local protests. John Patterson, the Madison police chief, acknowledged the shooting at a news conference, but stressed footage circulating was just one perspective of what happened. “I want to take a moment to honestly and sincerely offer my heartfelt sympathies to his family for this loss,” Patterson said. The dead man was in his 30s, Patterson said, but police did not release his name. The shooting happened on a city street in a popular neighborhood lined with restaurants, bars, shops and residential homes less than 2 miles from the state capitol. Footage of the incident shows numerous cars stopped in the intersection with people watching as it unfolded. “Another Black man is dead on a Madison street, and our community watched him die before we even knew his name,” said Brandi Grayson, the head of Urban Triage, a Madison-based Black community advocacy group. “That is the cruelty of state violence.” A crowd formed near where the shooting occurred on Wednesday with people chanting: “Arrest the cops!” Police had been responding to calls that someone was attempting to enter parked vehicles in the neighborhood, Patterson said. The suspect fled on a bike through nearby backyards in an attempt to get away before officers contacted him in the street, Patterson said. The suspect either fell off his bike or was taken off his bike by officers and during the struggle pulled out a knife and injured an officer, Patterson said. In one of the videos circulating online, three officers are seen holding the man down and attempting to arrest him. The officers lose control of the suspect, who frees his arms and attempts to get up off his knees. One officer appears to reach for his pistol, when a female officer runs toward the others shouting: “Taser! Taser!” That officer did not draw his firearm. After some jostling, the suspect falls to the ground. As he goes down, another officer rises to his feet, pulls out his pistol, leans down toward the man on the ground and fires three shots at close range. The officer who fired the shots then re-holsters his gun and places the suspect’s arm behind his back, before standing up and walking away. Two other officers push the suspect over onto his belly, before others arrive to attempt to offer medical attention. The officer who fired the lethal shots had been injured by the suspect with a knife, Patterson said. He described the officer as a veteran of the police force, but didn’t provide his name. Another officer was injured, but it’s not clear how, Patterson said. The other two officers were uninjured. The race of the officer who fired his gun on Wednesday and that of the man killed were not released by police. But advocates for the Black community in Madison said the man killed was Black. Sabrina Madison, the city council president, called the videos “both disturbing and painful”, in a statement. “I’ve received several calls from alders with questions and what I can say without a doubt is that I expect a full, comprehensive, and transparent independent investigation into today’s shooting of a community member,” the statement reads. The Wisconsin department of justice criminal investigation will investigate the shooting because it involves police officers. A white police officer shot and killed Tony Robinson, who was biracial, on the same street in 2015, a shooting that spurred large protests. The district attorney did not file charges against police in that incident, determining the officer had been justified. The Associated Press contributed
centerLeftText: A Madison police officer shot and killed a man who had injured him with a knife while resisting arrest, the city's police chief said Wednesday. Police have not identified the man who was killed, saying only that he was in his 30s. Patterson stressed that the footage circulating was just one perspective of what happened. "I ask the community to recognize that there is other video that they have not seen, that's not circulating online, that will be reviewed as part of this investigation," he said. "So, to the extent that they can as we all navigate this trauma together, we try to remain calm and remember that not all perspectives are online right now." The race of the officer who fired his gun on Wednesday and that of the man killed were not released by police. But advocates for the Black community in Madison said the man killed was Black. "That is the cruelty of state violence." Gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong speaks alongside protesters near where a Madison police officer shot and killed a man Wednesday, July 22, 2026, in Madison, Wis. AP Photo/Kayla Wolf A crowd formed near where the shooting occurred on Wednesday, with some people chanting, "Arrest the cops!" Among the protesters who gathered throughout the night was Wisconsin gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong. "We're gonna make sure that we're able to support any assembly that might happen, any protest that might happen, but also not wanting to amplify any issues or create friction where it's unnecessary, frankly, so we'll be reaching out to community members and community leaders to hopefully strike the right balance," Patterson said. Wisconsin Gov. Tony Evers said in a social media post he was "briefed on the officer-involved shooting that occurred in Madison today" and continues "to receive updates as more information is released." "There must be transparency and accountability in any investigation moving forward," Evers said in the post. Police were responding to calls that someone was attempting to enter parked vehicles in the neighborhood, Patterson said. The suspect fled on a bike through nearby backyards in an attempt to get away before officers contacted him in the street, the police chief said. The suspect either fell off his bike or was taken off his bike by officers, and during the struggle pulled out a knife and injured an officer, Patterson said. An officer deployed a taser but it was unsuccessful, the chief said. The same officer who was injured by the knife fired his weapon and the man who was shot later died at the hospital, the police chief said. He described the officer who fired his weapon as a veteran officer, but did not reveal any other details. "When a knife is produced during officers' apprehension arrest, the overall situation is clearly elevated. It becomes incredibly dangerous and it obviously increases risks for everyone there," Patterson said. "For every officer. For the person as well. I think it severely narrows the scope of options and possible outcomes." "This was not a small, folding knife. It's a large, fixed-blade knife," Patterson added, telling reporters a photo of the knife would eventually be shared. "My understanding is there are some witnesses that also saw this, so I think we gotta, as a community, recognize we don't have all the information right now, and we have to allow our investigators to piece things together and get as clear a picture as we can," he said. Another officer was injured, but it's not clear how, Patterson said, noting police don't believe that officer's injuries were caused by the knife. The other two officers were uninjured. All four officers will be placed on administrative leave, as is standard protocol for the department, during the investigation. The Wisconsin Department of Criminal Investigation will investigate the shooting because it involves police officers.

House Judiciary Committee Chairman Jim Jordan (R-OH) on Wednesday issued a criminal referral of former special counsel Jack Smith to the Department of Justice over allegations he lied to Congress about tracking personal phone messages of dozens of lawmakers.

Republicans have long raised concerns about Smith’s time at President Joe Biden’s DOJ. Smith obtained subpoenas that allowed him to access communications records of mostly Republican lawmakers as part of the DOJ’s and FBI’s “Arctic Frost” investigation. Last week, Senate Judiciary Committee Chairman Chuck Grassley (R-IA) released data he said indicated Smith’s team reviewed text messages from 44 senators and representatives from both parties, though mostly Republican.

During testimony before the House Judiciary Committee last December, Smith denied that he had examined the contents of lawmakers’ text messages. Jordan said in a letter this week to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche that he is submitting the criminal referral due to what he described as new evidence that Smith committed perjury. A DOJ official confirmed to the Washington Examiner that the department “will investigate all evidence of criminal conduct” in the referral.

Advertisement

“All individuals have an obligation to comply with a duly authorized congressional inquiry. Mr. Smith, a former public servant, is no different,” Jordan wrote in the letter, reported by Fox News. “Yet his testimony included intentionally false statements designed to obstruct the Committee’s oversight.” “While Mr. Smith testified that he did not seek a search warrant from telecommunications carriers for the content of Members’ text messages, he failed to disclose that his office received text message content from [the National Archives and Records Administration] relating to White House personnel phone records,” Jordan wrote, accusing Smith of having “excluded information that was highly relevant to the Committee’s examination.” During his House testimony last year, Smith claimed his team only sought telephone toll records. Advertisement “Toll records were sought for historical telephone routing information, collected after calls had taken place, identifying the incoming and outgoing call numbers, the time of the calls, and their duration,” he said. “Toll records do not include the content of calls.” Smith is known for leading the Jan. 6, 2021, election-interference and classified-documents cases against President Donald Trump. The Arctic Frost investigation was triggered by Smith’s prosecution of Trump due to alleged election interference in 2020 in the Jan. 6 case. Cruz has said Smith sent subpoenas to AT&T for his cellphone records, voicemails, text messages, and geolocation data. BILL PULTE IS URGED TO REVOKE SECURITY CLEARANCES FOR EX-JACK SMITH TEAM MEMBERS Advertisement Before saying last week that records prove Smith’s team reviewed personal text messages of 44 lawmakers, Grassley had said Smith issued 197 subpoenas to 34 individuals and 163 businesses, including financial institutions, as part of Arctic Frost, in conjunction with former FBI Director Christopher Wray. The subpoenas requested records and communications related to over 430 individuals and organizations, ”all of them appear to be aimed at Republicans,” according to Grassley. Cruz said last November that, in the investigation, U.S. District Judge James Boasberg signed off on subpoenas from Smith’s office that held “no basis,” ordering phone companies to allow the government to monitor the phone lines of nine Republican senators, including himself. Verizon complied with the subpoenas, while AT&T pushed back. “When you have the Biden Department of Justice subpoenaing 20% of the Republicans in the Senate, it is an egregious abuse of process,” Cruz said. “It is a fishing expedition, and we need to have a full and thorough investigation and accountability for every executive branch official who signed off on weaponizing the government.”

centerText:
The Houthis named the tankers as the Encelia and the Layla, though their responsibility has not been confirmed.

The group's military spokesperson Yahya Saree said they had targeted the ships with drones and missiles for allegedly violating its naval blockade.

Saree added that the Houthis promised to continue their naval operations and would continue "enforcing the 'siege for a siege' equation".

The state-run Saudi Press Agency confirmed early on Thursday that the Encelia was struck "while sailing in the Red Sea", resulting in a fire on its bow.

Citing an unnamed official source at the Transport General Authority, it described the attack as a violation of international law. All crew were safe, it reported.

UK Maritime Trade Operation (UKMTO) reported an "unknown projectile" struck a Saudi tanker about 130km from Al Shuqaiq in Saudi Arabia at 20:00 GMT. The attack caused a fire but no casualties, it said.

British maritime risk management group Vanguard also reported that the Saudi tanker Encelia had been hit.

The attack on the Layla has not been confirmed.

US Central Command (Centcom) said at 21:30 GMT it had launched strikes at Trump's direction "to further degrade Iran's ability to threaten civilian mariners and commercial vessels transiting regional waters".

The strikes targeted "Iranian military targets including maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites, and air defense assets," it said.

Kuwait, Jordan and Bahrain soon reported responding to airborne threats, before Iran's military said they had targeted US assets in all three countries, according to AFP.

Iranian state media reported two people had been killed in US strikes on the country's south near its border with Iraq.

Meanwhile, the Iranian Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said the Strait of Hormuz was "completely closed" after an explosion in the channel, Iranian state media reported.

The IRGC said it had stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait.

One tanker caught fire and two others turned back after attempting to pass through a mined route south of the strait, according to Iranian state media.

It comes after Trump promised earlier on Wednesday to attack an Iranian bridge or power plant if its forces hit ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi later warned of an "eye for an eye" response to attacks on Iran.

On Tuesday, Trump told reporters he would "take care of the Houthis" should they obstruct the Red Sea as they had said.

Few details of the "maritime embargo" were given by the Houthis when it was announced on Monday, but a media official said they were closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait - the Red Sea's southern gateway - to Saudi vessels.

left-center.title: Hitler’s Birthplace in Austria Is Now a Police Station

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.

centerText: Austria has opened a police station in the house where Adolf Hitler was born in the town of Braunau am Inn. It follows years of controversy about what to do with the 17th-Century former inn, where Nazi dictator Adolf Hitler was born in April 1889. The building has now been substantially refurbished and its facade changed, but it has been a huge headache for Austrian authorities, who want to prevent it from becoming a site of neo-Nazi pilgrimage. Hitler only lived in Salzburger Vorstadt 15 for a few weeks before his family moved to another address in Braunau. They left the town for good when he was three years old. Hitler returned briefly to Braunau in 1938, on his way to Vienna, after he annexed Austria to Nazi Germany. The opening ceremony for the police station, featuring police musicians playing a tuba and other brass instruments, was attended by Interior Minister Gerhard Karner and other local dignitaries. Austria was taking "responsibility" for the "terrible legacy" of the past, said Karner. While deciding what to do with the building had not been easy, Karner believed the police station marked "a new chapter" for it. "This house will now become a place of democracy, rule of law, security, freedom and human dignity," he said. A Catholic police chaplain then blessed two crosses which will hang in the new police station. For decades, the interior ministry had rented the building from its former owner, a local woman called Gerlinde Pommer. It paid her a generous rent, in an attempt to prevent the house being used as a site for extreme right-wing tourism.

topic: Tropical Storm Bertha Louisiana Moves Heads

left-center.title: Tropical Storm Bertha moves into Louisiana, heads for Texas. See its path.

center.title: News Wrap: Tropical Storm Bertha makes landfall in Louisiana

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText:
Tropical Storm Bertha was turning toward Texas early Thursday after making landfall over Louisiana on Wednesday, then heading offshore again. It continued to bring rain to a large swath of the northern Gulf Coast.

Bertha was moving westward off the Louisiana coast and "is expected to make landfall along the northwestern Gulf Coast today," the National Hurricane Center said, adding that the storm was traveling at 13 mph.

"Bertha is forecast to maintain tropical storm intensity up until it reaches the upper Texas coast later today," then "is expected to dissipate inland over Texas by tonight or Friday," the Miami-based center said.

Map shows projected path

Bertha, the second named storm of the Atlantic hurricane season, had maximum sustained winds of 45 mph after a gradual decrease since Tuesday night.

CBS News

"On the forecast track, the center of Bertha will move near or along the Louisiana and upper Texas coasts through this afternoon, and then move farther inland into Texas this evening and tonight," the center said.

Louisiana state climatologist Jay Grymes said Wednesday he expected some pockets of flooding, but added, "With luck, this will be an in-and-out kind of situation." It appears to have been.

Warnings in effect

A tropical storm warning is posted for the Gulf Coast from the Terrebonne/Lafourche Parish line in Louisiana to Sargent, Texas.

"Bertha is expected to produce total rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches with isolated totals to 6 inches through tonight along the Texas Gulf Coast and South Texas. This rainfall may produce isolated flash flooding, especially in urban areas," the center cautioned.

The system could also produce storm surge, raising waters up to 3 feet in some locations along the coast.

Bertha comes weeks after the 2026 hurricane season's first named storm, Tropical Storm Arthur, which made landfall in June along the Gulf Coast of Texas.
centerText: In our news wrap Wednesday, Tropical Storm Bertha made landfall on the coast of Louisiana about 40 miles east of New Orleans, this year's cyclosporiasis outbreak in the U.S. is officially the worst on record and OpenAI says it's investigating an "unprecedented cyber incident" after one of its AI models went rogue and hacked another company. topic: British Woman Jailed Hong Kong Blackmail

left.title: British woman jailed in Hong Kong for blackmail after accusing banker of rape

center.title: British woman jailed for blackmail after accusing banker of rape in Hong Kong

farLeftText: A British woman who reported being raped to police in Hong Kong has been sentenced to six years in prison for blackmail and perverting the course of public justice. Isabel Rose, 26, from south-east London, travelled to Hong Kong in early 2024 to visit a British banker she had met while travelling in south-east Asia. “I came here with four days of clothes, not realising I would be here for two years,” Rose said while out on bail. “Somewhere along the line, quite early on, something went wrong.” The Hong Kong district judge Adriana Noelle Tse Ching said Rose had “not shown an iota of remorse” before handing down the sentence, and called the allegation “completely fabricated”. The events manager, who was first arrested in 2024, maintained throughout the proceedings that she was raped. Radd Seiger, the spokesperson for Rose, said: “Today is obviously a difficult day for Isabel and her mother, Aysha. We will now take advice from the legal team and consider all available options.” In court, Rose, dressed in a black jacket over a white top, looked across at supporters as the judge handed down the sentence. She appeared solemn and gestured to the public gallery as she was escorted out of the dock. The case centred on allegations that Rose demanded money from the British banker, who can not be named for legal reasons, after she accused him of raping her. Rose travelled to Hong Kong in January 2024 after meeting the banker on holiday in Thailand. They stayed in touch and he later invited her to Hong Kong and offered to pay for her flights. Rose said she was raped in his apartment about 12 hours after landing in Hong Kong and reported the allegation to police. Police initially arrested the man but later released him and arrested Rose. She was subsequently charged with blackmail and perverting the course of public justice. Forensic testing to establish the rape was inconclusive. In a 300-page judgment, the judge said she accepted the man’s account and rejected Rose’s evidence. The court heard that Rose had asked for £5,000 after the alleged sexual assault and later demanded £100,000 while threatening to report him to the police, claims Rose denied. Rose said the money was compensation that the man offered to cover flights, accommodation and medical costs. The judge said the escalating demands amounted to blackmail. Rose was released on bail after the arrest but was unable to leave Hong Kong for almost two years while awaiting trial. The family said they have spent tens of thousands of pounds trying to fight the case. Rose has maintained her innocence. View image in fullscreen Aysha Bell, Isabel Rose’s mother, reported the alleged rape to the Metropolitan police in London. The Met said it was ‘unable to pursue relevant lines of enquiry’. Photograph: Fung Hoi Kin/The Guardian The case also focused on WhatsApp messages exchanged between Rose and the man after the alleged assault. In one exchange, Rose wrote: “You violated me last night, I didn’t wanna have sex.” He responded: “I am deeply sorry. I was slightly drunk and I misread your signals, it’s no excuse and I know it doesn’t make it any better.” The man told the court he misunderstood Rose’s use of the word “violated” to refer to an emotional violation rather than an allegation of rape. He said he read the message in two parts. The judge said Rose had deleted some texts and used disappearing messages. She said the banker had been “nothing but kind to the defendant” and Rose had “taken advantage of his kindness, naivety, friendship and romantic interest”. Rejecting calls from Rose’s legal team for a lighter sentence, the judge described her conduct as “wicked” and “evil”. Campaigners said the case raised important questions about how post-sexual assault behaviour was assessed in court. They said unpredictable behaviour was evidence of trauma, not fabrication. Ngozi Fulani, the founder of the London charity Sistah Space, which supports women of African and Caribbean heritage affected by domestic violence and sexual abuse, said: “We live in a world where people think there is a structured way to respond to rape and sexual assault. That is not the case. What has happened here is that a victim has been blamed for being a victim.” Supporters cried as the judge handed down the sentence. Rose’s mother, Aysha Bell, who has spent much of the past two years in Hong Kong supporting her daughter, appeared teary-eyed in court. In 2024, Bell reported the alleged rape to the Metropolitan police in London. UK police can legally investigate sexual assault cases involving British nationals abroad. The Met opened a file, assigned a detective to the case and according to correspondence seen by the Guardian, named the banker as a suspect. A Met spokesperson said: “We take reports of this nature extremely seriously and remain committed to investigating all allegations thoroughly. However, we have been unable to pursue relevant lines of enquiry in relation to this particular allegation due to a lack of jurisdiction in the relevant country. Should circumstances change, we will pursue all relevant enquiries.” The Foreign Office said it was supporting a British woman in Hong Kong and was in contact with her family and the local authorities.

centerText: According to local press reports, Judge Adriana Tse sentenced her to five years in prison for blackmail and three years for perverting the course of justice, with one year to be served consecutively. Prosecutors argued that Rose fabricated the allegation in an attempt to extort £100,000 ($134,000) from the man, whose identity was withheld. Rose denied the charges throughout the trial and has consistently maintained that she was raped. Rose had travelled to Hong Kong on 31 January 2024 after meeting the man while in Thailand a month earlier, according to court documents. At the centre of the prosecution case were WhatsApp messages exchanged between the pair in the hours and days after the alleged rape on 1 February at his apartment. In one message sent the following morning, Rose wrote: "Are you annoyed I didn't want to have sex," to which the man replied: "What no of course not. Was impressed by your restraint." Later that day, Rose wrote: "You violated me last night, I didn't wanna have sex." He replied: "I'm really deeply sorry, I was slightly drunk and I misread your signals, it's no excuse and I know it doesn't make it any better." In a later exchange, Rose wrote: "You basically raped me." The man replied: "That's not what I'm into at all and I feel sick." The pair later exchanged messages about money. According to the written judgement of the verdict published in March, the man agreed to reimburse Rose for her travel and accommodation. In one message, Rose wrote: "Send me the right amount you owe me for everything. Think about that. Think carefully." He transferred £5,000 that day and tried to send a further £5,000. Rose subsequently wrote: "You've given 10% of what I'm owed. 100% of my soul gone." In a later message, the man wrote: "But I don't have 100k, I have no way of paying you that." Rose reported the rape to police on 3 February. The man was arrested and questioned before being released without charge. Rose was arrested the following day and later charged with blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.

topic: Cockroach Protests Indias Modi Promises Punish

left.title: ‘Cockroach’ protests: India’s Modi promises to punish corrupt officials

center.title: India's Modi government under growing pressure as 'cockroach' protests intensify

farLeftText: Narendra Modi has said he will set up “fast-track courts” to expedite the punishment of corrupt officials who leaked examination papers, as India’s prime minister seeks to quell the youth-led “cockroach” protest movement that has rocked Delhi and challenged his government. In his first public remarks on the movement, Modi said on X “Nothing is more important than the welfare and future of our youth … Those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared.” The offer of faster justice than usual from India’s clogged judicial system, which can take decades to deliver a verdict, was swiftly rejected by the Cockroach Janta party (CJP), the satirical name the youth protest movement coined after India’s top judge called unemployed youths “cockroaches”. Saurav Das, a CJP spokesperson, said: “It’s a Band-id. That’s not giving us accountability. There have been zero convictions so far all these years so this measure is of no use.”. A core demand of the movement is the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, along reform of the education system. An estimated 50,000 people protested in Delhi on Monday, even though police denied them permission to march on parliament and later mounted baton charges and fired teargas. On Wednesday, more than 1,000 people remained camped out at the CJP’s protest site in central Delhi despite the gruelling heat and humidity. Many protesters had travelled for miles to be there. Renuka Chowdhury, an opposition MP with the Congress party, told the India Today news channel that no one could fathom why Modi would not sack Pradhan. “Honestly is this one minister more valuable, more important than restoring harmony and order to the country?” he said. Modi’s statement follows indications the government is softening its stand somewhat. Ministers have been at pains to say they are willing to talk to the CJP but the one thing they refuse to mention is Pradhan’s resignation. The protest site at Jantar Mantar is overflowing with supporters eager to show their solidarity. Unable to fit into the enclosed area, the crowd has spilled outside on to the roads. Some sort out donations of food and water sent by well wishers, others hand out food, including to the police on duty, fan those wilting from the heat, or clear up litter. Young people in other cities across India have also voiced their support for the CJP’s demands by holding their own demonstrations. There were three consecutive days of demonstrations in Mumbai. Chennai, Indore, Hyderabad, Patiala – also had solidarity protests. At a press conference on Wednesday, the opposition leader, Rahul Gandhi, escalated the rhetoric, demanding that apart from Pradhan resigning, Modi should apologise for failing to provide a fair education system. View image in fullscreen Demonstrators hold anti-Modi placards during a protest. Photograph: Ashish Vaishnav/SOPA Images/Shutterstock Gandhi said the country had seen 152 exam paper leaks over the past decade without resulting in a single conviction. “Somebody, or a group of people, are destroying our education system, India’s most valuable asset, destroying the lives of our students. What does the government do? Zero convictions,” he said. The health minister, JP Nadda, accused Gandhi of trying to make political gains from the protest. “Your concern is not to improve the education system, enhance the quality of education, or ensure justice for students. Instead, you are trying to use students’ issues and their demands for political mileage. You are trying to derive political benefit from them, and that is unfortunate,” he said. CJP leaders were deeply disappointed when their meeting on Monday with Nadda – the first in over 60 days – resulted in nothing. Its founder, Abhijeet Dipke, has since said that if ministers want to speak to them, they will have to come to the protest site and talk to them in public. Many in India are asking why Pradhan will not resign. Anish Gawande, an opposition politician, said Modi regarded backing down as weakness, while the prime minister no longer had the absolute majority he had in parliament in his first two terms. “He is facing global headwinds that prevent him from executing any major reforms in agriculture, energy, the economy. The unemployment rate is climbing to levels not seen in three decades,” he said. “A show of weakness now will give an entry point for the opposition to move in and build a similar momentum around other issues […] Before you know it, things have spiralled out of control.”
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: On Monday, tens of thousands of protesters flooded central Delhi after the CJP called for a march to parliament as the monsoon session began. At least 60 people were injured after police used batons and tear gas to disperse the crowds. Officers also dismantled the stage and makeshift tents at Jantar Mantar, the protest's main base near the 300-year-old observatory. But the demonstrators have refused to leave, maintaining a round-the-clock sit-in. A day after the violence, they were visited by several senior opposition leaders such as former Delhi chief minister Arvind Kejriwal and veteran politician Sharad Pawar. Later on Tuesday, Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge and Gandhi led a surprise march to Modi's house, demanding the resignations of Pradhan, Home Minister Amit Shah who the Delhi police report to and PM Modi. Gandhi said the government must apologise to students for the crackdown, adding that the Congress decided to stage a protest outside Modi's house because they weren't allowed to raise the issue in the parliament. He said he had met speaker Om Birla to seek a discussion, but "the speaker said he needed to take permission from the government, but it was very clear to us that there was no interest in the government of having a debate". Federal Minister Jitendra Singh denied Gandhi's allegation, external - he said he informed the Congress MP that the government had accepted his demand for a discussion but alleged that Gandhi later also demanded the resignation of Pradhan. He accused Gandhi of "changing the goalpost" and of refusing to end the protest despite being told that the place wasn't conducive to such demonstrations.

topic: Police Officer Rapist Attacked Women Scottish

left.title: Scottish police officer was rapist who attacked women over 14 years

center.title: Police officer was violent rapist who attacked women over 14 years, BBC reveals

farLeftText: A police officer who took his own life in May was a rapist who attacked at least nine women over 14 years. PC Alan Greer’s offending came to light when his first known victim, a woman he raped in her home in Glasgow in 2012 after she had called the police to report being the victim of a crime, reported the attack late last year. Her disclosure prompted one of Police Scotland’s biggest anti-corruption inquiries, which so far has uncovered a further eight victims, five of whom were sex workers, whom Greer attacked while off duty. A BBC Disclosure investigation has revealed that Greer used hundreds of sex workers throughout his police career, and it is anticipated more victims of his violence will be identified. A source close to the investigation told the BBC the case resembled that of a Met Police officer who was jailed for the rape and murder of Sarah Everard in London five years ago, after missed opportunities to catch his offending. They said: “It feels a bit like this could be Police Scotland’s Wayne Couzens moment.” Assistant chief constable Lynn Ratcliff, gold commander for the investigation, confirmed that the inquiry had found “truly shocking” evidence. She said: “This officer has clearly abused his power and has revisited someone that he’s encountered in the course of his duties. But this victim was brave enough to come and speak to us, and that really was the trigger point for us starting our investigation.” Greer, who was born in Australia in 1978, joined Strathclyde police in 2009 and had 17 years of apparently unblemished service. When he died in May he was 47, with a wife and child. Greer’s family and colleagues are understood to have been oblivious to his violent double life. Senior officers insisted there were no opportunities to spot his offending sooner. After the woman reported the sex attack that happened in 2012, the case was handed to the anti-corruption unit (ACU), which was able to identify the call she’d made in 2012 and the officers who had attended. They found that, in the weeks after the attack, Greer had repeatedly monitored the woman’s crime file on the police computer for updates. Anti-corruption detectives obtained his mobile phone number, which was then put through police intelligence databases, revealing a single piece of intelligence dating back to 2018, linked to that number, but with no name attached. The intelligence was from a Glasgow sex worker and indicated that the owner of that phone number was a customer who had been violent. This gave detectives grounds to monitor every keystroke that Greer had made on police computers, where they discovered that Greer had been searching the police system for traces of a complaint from a phone number belonging to a Glasgow sex worker. A sheriff authorised a warrant to search his home and seize his devices, where officers found thousands of graphic videos and images, revealing that Greer had used hundreds of sex workers throughout his police career. Officers are not yet even halfway through the files, but have already identified five sex workers who were seriously sexually assaulted and two female police officers Greer had taken inappropriate photographs of while they were training. If Greer were alive, it is understood police would have sought charges of attempted murder in some of the cases due to the level of violence. As Greer’s serial offending was emerging, social work officers, accompanied by local police, visited his home on 5 May to ensure his family were not in danger. Greer died by suicide the following day. Ratcliff said that no red flags were missed, but added that Greer would not pass vetting checks today. Information and support for anyone affected by rape or sexual abuse issues is available from the following organisations. In the UK, Rape Crisis offers support on 0808 500 2222 in England and Wales, 0808 801 0302 in Scotland, or 0800 0246 991 in Northern Ireland. In the US, Rainn offers support on 800-656-4673. In Australia, support is available at 1800Respect (1800 737 732). Other international helplines can be found at ibiblio.org/rcip/internl.html
